title: Sunday, March 11
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
March 11, 2018 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fourth Sunday in Lent (Laetare Sunday), falling within the season of Lent leading toward Easter (April 1, 2018). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included John 3:14-21 (the Nicodemus passage with "God so loved the world") as the Gospel, paired with Numbers 21:4-9 (the bronze serpent) and Psalm 107:1-3, 17-22. Laetare Sunday traditionally brings a slight lightening of Lenten austerity, with rose-colored vestments often appearing instead of purple.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of General Convention preparations and ongoing debates about marriage and sexuality that had dominated the previous triennium. Bishop Robert Wright of the Diocese of Colorado was serving in his role overseeing the cathedral and diocesan operations. No major diocesan elections or significant Colorado Episcopal news appears to have occurred in the week immediately preceding this sermon, though the national church continued navigating post-2015 marriage equality implementation.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and occasional snow possible in the mountains. The city was focused on the 2018 midterm election cycle, with Colorado governor's race positioning beginning to intensify around this time. No major breaking news events appear to have dominated the Denver Metro area's headlines in the immediate week before March 11, though the city remained engaged with typical early-spring community and political activity.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The week of March 11, 2018 was dominated by the ongoing Mueller investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election, with Paul Manafort facing money laundering indictments. The Parkland school shooting aftermath continued to shape national discourse around gun control, with student activism and congressional testimony still in the headlines from late February. March Madness NCAA basketball tournament was in its opening rounds, capturing significant American sports attention.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Syrian civil war continued with ongoing humanitarian crisis and Russian military involvement, though no single major escalation appears to have occurred in this specific week. The Korean Peninsula was in a period of diplomatic engagement following the PyeongChang Winter Olympics in February, with North and South Korea in preliminary talks about denuclearization. The Skripal poisoning incident in the UK (involving a former Russian spy) from March 4 was still developing as a major story, with UK-Russia tensions escalating.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in March 2018 was characterized by heightened political polarization and generational activism around gun violence, with Parkland survivors' voices dominating social media and news cycles. The #MeToo movement continued reshaping entertainment and workplace accountability discussions from its 2017 emergence. Sports culture was focused on March Madness basketball, while film audiences were engaged with Black Panther's massive box office success and cultural impact from its February release.
